behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: A pure delay-calculation unit test can miss wiring bugs (wrong retry counter, cap applied at wrong point, or scheduler not using computed delay). A test should execute the retry loop with controlled failures and a fake clock across retry/orchestrator modules.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real retry policy + retry executor + timer abstraction. Use a stub transport that fails N times. Advance a fake clock and assert observed scheduled delays are 1x, 2x, 4x... capped at max, based on actual retry attempts.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Failure often sits at the HTTP boundary (status code, headers like Retry-After, casing/format parsing). In-process mocks can hide real protocol/serialization behavior. Real HTTP exchange is the minimum reliable boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Run against a local HTTP test server that returns real 429 responses with representative headers/body variants. Verify the client classifies them as rate-limit events and schedules the wait derived from response metadata/backoff rules.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This validates end-to-end request replay correctness over HTTP (method/path/body/headers preserved) plus retry timing. A pure mock may pass even if real request reconstruction is wrong.
behaviors.test_description: Use a local HTTP server scripted to return 429 once, then 200. Capture both incoming requests and assert the second is a true resend of the original. Assert `call()` resolves with the success payload without exposing retry internals to the caller.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: The key risk is error mapping after retry exhaustion across retry counter + classifier + error factory. No OS/network dependency is required if failures are deterministically injected.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real retry stack with a transport stub that always yields rate-limit failures. Configure max retries, execute `call()`, and assert rejection is exactly `QuotaExhaustedError` (type/fields), not a generic or raw transport error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is a cross-module control-flow safety property (retry loop + quota tracker + stop condition). A unit test of one function can miss emergent loop bugs; real external infra is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full client pipeline with deterministic failing responses and fake time. Assert total attempts == 1 + maxRetries, terminal error is surfaced, and no further tasks/timers remain queued after completion.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is shared in-process state under concurrency (race conditions, per-instance leakage, non-atomic updates). This requires multiple callers and real module wiring, but not OS/network realism.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/account context. Fire many concurrent `call()` invocations with a controlled transport. Assert aggregate quota consumption is global across callers (not per-caller), and limit enforcement behavior is consistent under contention.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering guarantees depend on queue implementation + async scheduler interaction. Unit tests of queue helpers can miss integration bugs where dispatcher reorders under timing pressure.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple calls in known order while transport/quota forces waiting. Release capacity stepwise and record send order at the transport boundary. Assert strict FIFO by submission timestamp/sequence ID.
